Issue No.1 2 • 	July 2012
How To Blatandy Pitch Your Product
In A Way Where Your Subscribers
Love Every Single Word Of It
(And Happily Buy, Too, 	Of Course ... )
Greetings Mr. Email Player,
I'm usually not one to suggest doing constant blatant pitching in your emails. A
few people can get away with it (like marketing legend jim Straw -- his emails are pure
pitch yet I still like getting them ... ) But, that doesn't mean you can't do it and still not lose
half your list in the process. You just have to use a bit of strategy, finesse and curiosity. In
fac'L you have to crank up the curiosity factor 10 fold AND Dlake absolutely
100°/o certain your readers really want what you're teasing. If you do it right,
you'll often find your sales exploding through the roof with hardly anyone caring you're
')ust pitching" at all. On the other hand, if you do it wrong, you'll find your sales staying
stagnant and eventually turning off even your most dedicated fans (yes, fans, you're not
building a "list" you're building a fan base.) So how do you do it right and avoid doing it
wrong? Well, it's tough to explain. It's something you get a "feel" for after writing lots and
lots of emails, and having that dialogue with your list (another reason to be mailing
DAILY, cupcake). But, even if I can't explain it to you, what I can do is SHOW you some
examples. Here's one that kicked gluteus assimus. Note how it's a blatant pitch, but it's an

Hey, 	check this out:
A friend recently asked me about some of the tips in my 	(Name Of

book. 	Specifically, 	the part where I 	talk about how to lose fat
simply by changing the way you shower.
How is this possible?
IS it even possible?
The answer is yes!
I've done it many times.
And so have my readers.
But, 	here's the thing:
You have to do it in a very specific way I 	talk about in the book.
Just taking a shower obviously isn't enough 	(or else everyone would
be thin ••• ). 	No, 	you have to set it at a specific temperature
and do it for a specific amount of time.
It's one of dozens of unorthodox 	(but 100% proven) 	tips inside.
That's why I 	tell people:
Don't even bother reading it if you don't have an open mind.
They ARE strange tips.
But they work like crazy.
To see more of the tips go to:
(WEBSITE URL)
SIGNATURE
OK, let's put this baby under the microscope and see what makes it "tick." For

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
one, I do tell them WHAT to do (take showers). But obviously, that information is not
enough to go on to lose weight (otherwise the entire shower-taking world would be thin
and not need our product in the first place). So it's interesting and intriguing, even though
it tells them nothing they can really use. It's also short, too. I mean, no need to drag it out
forever. And finally, I purposely used the "take away" at the end (don't use if you don't
have an open mind, etc). I think this is important if you can work it in when doing blatant
pitches. A good take away "softens" the obnoxious nature of a pitch in a way few other
things can (adding proof and credibility can do the same -- see the next example -- but
the take away is so much more ... fun ... ).
Anyway, let's take a look at another example...
Subject Line: Computer scientist discovers quick list building secret
{lfirstname_fix},
Let's rap about list building, 	shall we?
It's quite frustrating for most people. 	But for the past few months
I've been experimenting with a way to rapidly build my list that
ANYONE can do.
It takes about 5-minutes 	(give or take).
And is nabbing more subscribers than ever.
(Spanning multiple niches.)
I 	learned this trick from a computer scientist who stumbled onto it
while doing PPC for a client, 	and noticed a huge swelling of new
opt ins coming through with JUST their existing traffic.
How does it work?
Basically, 	it goes like this:
You write your capture pages in a way where the reader perceives
time passing and they'll miss hopping on the train 	(i.e. 	missing
out) 	if they don't opt in right NOW.
This is NOT about using scarcity or anything gimmicky.
It's purely a language thing.

difference? This one was a blatant pitch. True, it was somewhat informational (when

in this one, too, of course. Anyway, what's the moral of the story? Don't be afraid to
blatantly pitch. Just make sure you do it in a way where people want to come back for
more ... (which, really, you want to do with EVERY email). Master the art of curiosity and
teasing and taking it away and you can pitch to your heart's content. More on ways to do
this in future "Email Players" issues (we haven't even scratched ye old surface yet, amigo).
Teaching vs Selling:
Part 2 Of Infinity
Let us continue our journey from last month into the whole "soft teaching" vs
"hard teaching" affair. To recap (for new subscribers): If you hard teach in your emails
you will likely not make nearly as much of the green stuff than if you do what I call "soft
teach." This is where you make your emails informational and interesting and
entertaining, but you don't really teach them anything that's in your product. It sells them
on wanting your product but doesn't look, feel or smell like you're trying to sell them
anything (at least until you make your call to action ...) Anyway, I said I'd spend a lot of
time on this and I intend to keep my word on that. This is arguably one of the MOST
important email writing skills you can develop, and there are many ways of doing it. One
way is to tell them how they can use and apply your product after they've bought it. I call
these "application" emails. And it's just like future-pacing, but in a more informational
way. Again this FEELS like teaching, but it's selling. And, when you do it right, it will sell
the living shee-yat out of your products.
OK, now for some examples:
Here's another email that got more new "Email Players" subscribers than normal
(remember, I'm selling high ticket continuity, so when I show you these examples, you can
-5-

Or ••• 	or ••• 	you get the idea.

Can't find a time machine?
Not even on eBay?

3. 	Start building an email list and mailing it.

Notice how this email does not teach anything about email marketing, but instead
just illustrates how it can help improve their lives? All I'm "teaching" is fundamental
concepts like mail daily, build a list, etc-- but I don't tell them HOW to do these things.
For that information, they need to subscribe to my newsletter (where I promptly show
them these things each month). Also, another way to be informational (but not hard
educational) is to tell them what NOT to do. In this case, I had some fun with it by telling
them to kiss their asses goodbye and fmd a time machine. But these things simply position
my REAL solution (email) as the only viable option. Think of ways you can do this. It can
help you sell far more of your products and services. Finally, notice how this email is the
answer to a question that is VERY common in my market. It's probably one of the most-
asked questions there is ("how can I get started and make money fast?") If you can find
your market's most-asked questions then you can go to town on tltese kinds of emails.
Let's look at another example of an application email that kicked bootay -- way
more sales than usual:
Subject Line: 	"My husband told me he's embarrassed by my size"
Hi,
A little while back, 	I 	got this subscriber email:
"I have been dieting for MONTHS and have been losing weight
steadily from 350 lbs to now being around 320 lbs. 	But my husband
today decided to tell me he is embarrassed by mel 	I 	don't know what
to do. 	I 	am lost! 	The person who I 	assumed loved me no matter what
has knocked the wind out of me emotionally. 	I 	am really motivated
now to lose my extra weight but don't know how to do it any faster.
I 	am going to go cry for a while now. 	This is 	just so hard. 	But I
wanted to know your opinion?"
Well, 	I'll stay out of the husband part.

First off, this is obviously a very emotional email going for maximum angst and
fear (this makes marketers... uncomfy... sometimes, but the story in this email is absolutely
real, and it is a major insecurity for our list, it'd be foolish not to write an email about it).
Anyway, where was I? Oh yes, now, notice how the email basically tells them to use the
product and exercise. But it's done in an "advice" like format -- again, telling them to
apply the information and watch what happens. It's informational, but not hard teaching.
It's selling. And selling, my fine furry friend, will get you m.ore sales than
spilling your candy in the lobby (i.e. giving away your infonnation free). So
give this kind of email a try -- several times. Watch what happens. Me thinks you 'II be one
smiling mo' fo' when you use it.
Hey, how about now we switch gears and talk about list building. I have been doing
something the last few months that has doubled (yes doubled) my opt ins. And the beauty
of it is, anyone (I don't care who you are or what you sell) can do the same.
Ready?
OK thenjoker, here. we. GO--
''Splash'' 	On A Tidal Wave
Of New Opt-Ins
I believe this one tip ALONE (if you em.ail aggressively and sm.artl~
and irnplem.ent what you learn here) will pay for the next 10 years of your
"Em.ail Players" subscription. There's absolutely nothing "new" about the
following. In fact, I remember people doing this years ago. But for some reason it was
replaced by the squeeze page as the best way to collect opt ins. However, since Google
started spanking everyone, the new thing is now a "splash squeeze page." I read this back
in February in Dan Kennedy's newsletter in an article Ryan Deiss wrote. He was saying
how this is what the White House uses (not that that matters all that much) and to test it.
Well, I tested it and low and behold-- it doubled my opt ins pretty much overnight. The
results have just been crazy. (And that has obviously not hurt sales, yo?)

THE 	EMAIL 	PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
Again, it's not "new" by any means.
But if you haven't been using one, you're really missing out on opt ins.
Basically what a splash squeeze page does is it asks for the opt in, but it also clearly
gives readers the option to say "no thanks" and sally forth into the rest of your site
without opting in (of course, once inside I go all out and keep trying to capture their
emails --light box pop ups, side bar forms, etc). Plus, I've also noticed something else
that's kind of interesting. This MIGHT just be coincidence (or maybe it's just unique to
my site for some reason I cannot fathom) ... but I've also noticed FAR more of the opt ins
via this method are double confirming than they used to. So if you have double
confirmation opt in forms, this is especially worth testing.
Best part:
Just about any website guyI gal can hook this up for you cheaply!
One of my friends did mine in about 5 minutes, and he's since now started offering
this to his clients after seeing the results. The only bummer about this is it's getting more
and more popular. Which means more and more people will be using it. Which means it
probably won't work as well eventually (and we'll have to think up a new format ...)
But until then, go for it!
To see an example of how these pages can look, here are some links:
www.BenSettle.coiD
(of course ... )
www.WhiteHouse.gov
Anyway, I SHUDDER to think of all the opt-ins I've lost over the years by NOT
doing this or knowing about it. My opt ins have exploded over the past few months in part
due to this (I use awebcr and the vast majority of opt-ins come from my splash squeeze
page, hands down).

THE 	EMAIL PLAYERS 	NEWSLETTER
The Selling Power
Of High ''Contrast''
One of the coolest lessons I've learned (writing lessons) is the power of contrast.
Matt Furey taught it in his original email course. And I also got a lesson on this when
writing an ad for a PPC course -- where the author of the course said he was able to get
way more clicks by using contrast.
But, you may be asking, what do I mean by "contrast"?
Well, everything!
Contrasting ideas... contrasting sentence/paragraph lengths... contrasting words ...
contrasting colors... high contrast gets big attention which can put lots more sales in your
hot little hand. In fact, if you want to write emails people love reading (but can't really put
their finger on WHY they love reading them so much) incorporate high contrast into
everything you do online. At first you have to think about it. But the more you do it, the
more natural and "second nature" it gets -- until one day it's just automatic. You can't
even remember using contrast in your email, it's just there. Anyway, in this issue I'm going
to give you two examples of contrast for email. One is a list of contrasting word
combinations (i.e. "oxymorons"). And the other, a way to use contrast to make your
emails FAR easier to read (which will make them FAR more likely to get a click and,
ultimately, a sale... )
Let's start with the list of oxymorons.
Your homework assignment is to write an email using oxymorons like these (and
there are MANY more).Just get used to writing like this when it makes sense, don't try to
force it, everything should be natural -- like a conversation. Again, the more you do it, the
easier it gets. This is purely writing training now. But I have found it to be extremely
profitable to use. Alright enough build up, here's the list:
1. virtual reality
2. original copy
-12-

3. old news
4. act naturally
5. pretty ugly
6. living dead
7. jumbo shrimp
8. rolling stop
9. constant variable
10. exact estimate
11. paid volunteers
12. civil war
13. sound of silence
14. clever fool
15. only choice
Don,t worry if this is a bit hard at first. Make it a goal to write ONE oxymoron per
email for the rest of this week. You don,t have to do it in every email all the time -- but to
train your brain to start thinking this way ies good to do it as often as possible until you
don,t have to think about it anymore. Now, on to the next kind of contrast. Contrasting
paragraph and sentence sizes and shapes. This will make your emails way easier on the
eye to read. And so in addition to using one oxymoron in your next few emails, I also
want you to start playing around with "staggering, your paragraphs and sentence
structures.
Contrast them as much as possible. Here is how I explained it recently to an

"Email Players" subscriber:
The more sizes and shapes of paragraphs are varied up (contrasted) 	the
easier it looks to read in most cases. 	It's something I've been
constantly playing with and tweaking for the last couple years. 	Even do
it in my regular sales letters now, 	it 	just looks more inviting
(usually, 	but it's also kind of an artsy fartsy thing in the end, 	and
subjective)
So often what I do is:
short sentence like this
even shorter
Then a longer paragraph like this maybe with lots of words
and weight to it, 	maybe even several sentences compressed
together as long as they keep the same train of thought in
tact, 	and makes sense.
Then something short again.
Maybe now something a little bit longer.
Then a two line paragraph like this one where it's a long
sentence 	(or multiple short ones).
Then another one line.
Maybe another.
Oneword.
Then back to a longer more 	"heavier' 	paragraph that is two
lines or so like this
Etc etc etc
You can see examples of this in pretty much every single email I write. For
example, notice the high contrast paragraph/ sentence structure in this email:
Subject Line: 	Sociopathic "doctors" online
Hello,

I'm not a doctor.
Nor do I 	play one on the Internet.
Yet, 	every now and then, 	someone thinks I 	am a doctor and can
diagnose their prostate condition. 	But I 	can't and am not trained
to. 	And frankly, 	even if I WAS a doctor 	(and I'm not!) 	I 	still
could not diagnose you without seeing you in person, 	anyway.
So why do I 	bring this up?
Two reasons:
1. Sometimes people ask me specific medical advice 	(even going as
far as addressing me as 	"Doctor Settle")
2. Not everyone online is so honest
Scary, 	but true.
You might be shocked by how many hucksters there are online --
posing as doctors or medically trained specialists 	just to sell
their magic pill solutions.
Solutions that can potentially RUIN your health.
(Or worse!)
In fact, 	some people selling various health products are literally
sociopaths 	(they have no conscience!) 	and have no problem selling
utter crap or poison to make 	a quick buck.
And that's the point:
Be careful online.
Not everyone is the boogy man.
But they're out there.
And you really SHOULD check with your doctor before listening to
anyone else -- including the tips in my own eBook at:
http://www.ProstateProblemsFixed.com/ebook
Just a friendly public service announcement.

Anyway, you can practice this every time you write an email. just like with the
oxymorons, it may not be easy at first. In fact, you will probably have to really think about
it. But the more you do it, the easier it gets until it's just second nature -- something you
do without thinking at all (I barely even give it a second thought 99% of the time, it just
spills out this way -- the same will happen for you if you work at it).

How To Merge Your Email Skillz
With Mobile Marketing
Interview with Mike Do lpies
www.Mob il eMarketin gCMS .com
Ben: We're going to talk about something that I 'm completely ignorant on. In fa ct, 1 don't even
have a smart phone yet. I have one coming this week. I used to have an i Phone, but I never used
it except to check weather and as a glorified iPod, so I got rid of it. I've got an other one com ing.
1 want to ta lk to you about mob ile marketing and email markcLing an d how we can merge Lhese
[\VO things together and how rney can be merged LOgethei: People who are on my list arc a ll email
guys and gals, so it would be good to talk to it from their perspective.
The first question 1 want to ask you, just lor people like me who arc completely ignorant of th is
whole th ing, when you say mobile marketing, what exactly does that m ean as far as you're
concerned?
